passage: We have hard work ahead. There is no resting for any of us till we redeem our pledge in full fill we make all the people of India what destiny intends them to be. We are citizens of a great country, on the verge of bold advance, and we have to live up to that high standard. All of us, to whatever religion we may belong, are equally the children of India with, equal rights, privileges and obligations. We cannot encourage communalism or narrow mindedness, for no nation can be great whose people are narrow in thought or action.

questions.question: The challenge the author of the above passage throws to the public is to achieve
options: (a) a high standard of living, progress and privileges
(b) equal privileges, fulfilment of destiny and political tolerance
(c) spirit of adventure and economic parity
(d) hard work, brotherhood and national unity
questions.explanation: The author emphasizes hard work to 'redeem our pledge', equality regardless of religion (brotherhood), and overcoming communalism (national unity) [cite: 830-833, 838].

passage: "The individual, according to Rousseau, puts his person and all his power in common under the supreme direction of the General Will and in our corporate capacity we receive each member as an indivisible part of the whole." In a democratic State, where a high degree of Political maturity of the people obtains, the conflict between the will of the sovereign and the people seldom occurs.

questions.question: In the light of the above passage, the nature of General Will is best described as
options: (a) the sum total of the private wills of the individuals
(b) what is articulated by the elected representatives
(c) the collective good as distinct from the sum of individual wills
(d) the material interests of the community
questions.explanation: The passage describes the individual becoming an 'indivisible part of the whole' under the General Will, implying a collective identity beyond just a sum of parts[cite: 808, 825].

questions.question: What does the passage imply regarding the actual exercise of sovereignty?
options: (a) In a mature democracy, the conflict between the sovereign and the people is minimized.
(b) Sovereignty is a myth in modern democratic states.
(c) The General Will is irrelevant in the actual exercise of sovereignty.
(d) Sovereignty is narrowed down to a marginal phenomenon.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that in a state with high political maturity, the conflict between the will of the sovereign and the people 'seldom occurs'[cite: 798, 822].

passage: A successful democracy depends upon widespread interest and participation in politics, in which voting is an essential part. To deliberately refrain from taking such an interest, and from voting, is a kind of implied anarchy; it is to refuse one's political responsibility while enjoying the benefits of a free political society.

questions.question: This passage relates to
options: (a) duty to vote
(b) right to vote
(c) freedom to vote
(d) right to participate in politics
questions.explanation: The passage characterizes refraining from voting as a refusal of 'political responsibility', thereby framing voting as a duty[cite: 814, 844].

passage: In a free country, the man who reaches the position of leader is usually one of outstanding character and ability. Moreover, it is usually possible to foresee that he will reach such a position, since early in life one can see his qualities of character. But this is not always true in the case of a dictator; often he reaches his position of power through chance, very often through the unhappy state of his country.

questions.question: The passage seems to suggest that
options: (a) a leader foresees his future position
(b) a leader is chosen only by a free country
(c) a leader must see that his country is free from despair
(d) despair in a country sometimes leads to dictatorship
questions.explanation: The passage mentions that dictators often rise to power 'through the unhappy state of his country', which correlates to national despair[cite: 850, 857].
